1. Title and Student Information¶

Predictive Maintenance: Machine Failure Prediction Using Machine Learning¶

Student Name: Ozan Berk Aydin
Student Number: GH1046991
Course: M505 Intro to AI and Machine Learning
Submission Date: June 2026

Task: Binary classification
Dataset: Industrial Machine Predictive Maintenance, Kaggle

2. Introduction¶

This project predicts whether a machine will fail within 24 hours using sensor, operating and maintenance features. The target is failure_within_24h, so this is a binary classification task.

A useful model can reduce downtime and repair cost by warning maintenance teams before breakdowns happen.

3. Data Collection¶

The Kaggle Industrial Machine Predictive Maintenance dataset was downloaded manually and loaded from data/predictive_maintenance_v3.csv.

  • Observations: 24,042
  • Original variables: 15
  • Target: failure_within_24h
  • Source: https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/tatheerabbas/industrial-machine-predictive-maintenance
  • Usage: public Kaggle dataset; no separate local license file was found.
  • Project repository for reproducibility: https://github.com/ozanberkaydin/M505_Predictive_Maintenance_Project

The data is suitable because it has mixed feature types, missing values and a non-trivial supervised target.

In [1]:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings("ignore")

RANDOM_STATE = 42

raw_data= pd.read_csv("data/predictive_maintenance_v3.csv")
df = raw_data.copy()

df.head()

4. Exploratory Data Analysis¶

Initial Observations¶

The dataset has 24,042 rows and 15 variables. Several sensor fields have missing values, and both numerical and categorical features need preprocessing.

In [7]:
df.duplicated().sum()
Out[7]:
np.int64(0)
In [8]:
df["failure_within_24h"].value_counts()
Out[8]:
failure_within_24h
0    20482
1     3560
Name: count, dtype: int64
In [9]:
df["failure_within_24h"].value_counts(normalize=True) * 100
Out[9]:
failure_within_24h
0    85.19258
1    14.80742
Name: proportion, dtype: float64
In [10]:
plt.figure(figsize=(6,4))
sns.countplot(data=df, x="failure_within_24h")
plt.title("Target Variable Distribution")
plt.show()

The target is moderately imbalanced: about 85% normal observations and 15% failures within 24 hours. Therefore, F1-score, precision, recall and ROC-AUC are more informative than accuracy alone.

Categorical failure rates are checked for machine type and operating mode.

In [11]:
for cat in ["machine_type", "operating_mode"]:
    rates = df.groupby(cat)["failure_within_24h"].mean().sort_values(ascending=False)
    print(f"Failure rate by {cat}:")
    print(rates)
    plt.figure(figsize=(6,3))
    rates.plot(kind="bar")
    plt.title(f"Failure Rate by {cat}")
    plt.ylabel("Failure Rate")
    plt.xticks(rotation=15)
    plt.show()

In [12]:
selected_features = [
    "temperature_motor",
    "vibration_rms",
    "pressure_level",
    "rpm"
]

for feature in selected_features:
    plt.figure(figsize=(6,4))

    sns.histplot(
        data=df,
        x=feature,
        hue="failure_within_24h",
        kde=True
    )

    plt.title(f"Distribution of {feature} by Failure Status")
    plt.xlabel(feature)
    plt.ylabel("Count")

    plt.show()

Feature Distributions¶

Failure cases tend to have higher motor temperature and vibration values. This supports keeping sensor variables as predictors.

In [13]:
numeric_df = df.select_dtypes(include=["int64", "float64"])

plt.figure(figsize=(12,8))

sns.heatmap(
    numeric_df.corr(),
    annot=True,
    fmt=".2f",
    cmap="coolwarm"
)

plt.title("Correlation Heatmap of Numerical Features")

plt.show()

Correlation Analysis¶

estimated_repair_cost is highly correlated with the target, but it is removed before training because it is only known after failure. Among usable operational variables, temperature and vibration show clear positive relationships with failure risk.

In [14]:
selected_features = [
    "temperature_motor",
    "vibration_rms",
    "pressure_level",
    "rpm"
]

for feature in selected_features:

    plt.figure(figsize=(6,4))

    sns.boxplot(
        data=df,
        x="failure_within_24h",
        y=feature
    )

    plt.title(f"Boxplot of {feature} by Failure Status")

    plt.show()

Outlier Analysis¶

Boxplots show outliers in vibration, temperature and RPM. In predictive maintenance these may represent abnormal operating states, so they are retained rather than removed automatically.

Missing Values¶

Missing sensor values are handled inside the pipeline using imputation. This avoids dropping useful rows and reduces leakage risk.

In [15]:
missing_values = df.isnull().sum().sort_values(ascending=False)
missing_values[missing_values > 0]
Out[15]:
vibration_rms        1000
pressure_level        924
temperature_motor     834
current_phase_avg     731
rpm                   533
dtype: int64
In [16]:
plt.figure(figsize=(8,4))

missing_values[missing_values > 0].plot(kind="bar")

plt.title("Missing Values by Feature")
plt.ylabel("Count")
plt.show()

5. Data Cleaning and Feature Engineering¶

Before modelling, leakage fields are removed, missing values are imputed, categorical variables are one-hot encoded and numerical variables are scaled when needed.

In [17]:
X = df.drop(
    [
        "failure_within_24h",
        "failure_type",
        "estimated_repair_cost",
        "timestamp",
        "rul_hours"
    ],
    axis=1
)

y = df["failure_within_24h"]

Target Leakage Prevention¶

failure_type, estimated_repair_cost and rul_hours were removed because they contain information that would not be available before a failure decision. Keeping them produced unrealistically perfect scores.

In [18]:
X["machine_id"] = X["machine_id"].astype(str)
In [19]:
numeric_features = X.select_dtypes(
    include=["int64", "float64"]
).columns.tolist()

categorical_features = X.select_dtypes(
    include=["object"]
).columns.tolist()

print("Numerical Features:")
print(numeric_features)

print("\nCategorical Features:")
print(categorical_features)

Numerical Features:
['vibration_rms', 'temperature_motor', 'current_phase_avg', 'pressure_level', 'rpm', 'hours_since_maintenance', 'ambient_temp']

Categorical Features:
['machine_id', 'machine_type', 'operating_mode']
In [20]:
df["machine_id"].nunique()
Out[20]:
20

Feature Selection Decision¶

machine_id has 20 identifiers. It is kept as a categorical feature because repeated observations may capture machine-specific risk patterns without imposing an artificial numeric order.

In [21]:
df["machine_id"].head()
Out[21]:
0    1
1    1
2    1
3    1
4    1
Name: machine_id, dtype: int64
In [22]:
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X,
    y,
    test_size=0.20,
    random_state=RANDOM_STATE,
    stratify=y
)

print(X_train.shape)
print(X_test.shape)

(19233, 10)
(4809, 10)

Train-Test Split¶

An 80/20 train-test split is used with random_state=42. Stratification preserves the failure rate in both sets, which is important for the imbalanced target.

Preprocessing Pipeline¶

In [23]:
from sklearn.impute import SimpleImputer
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler, OneHotEncoder
from sklearn.feature_selection import SelectKBest, f_classif
from sklearn.pipeline import Pipeline
from sklearn.compose import ColumnTransformer
from sklearn.linear_model import LogisticRegression
from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeClassifier
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier, GradientBoostingClassifier
from sklearn.model_selection import cross_val_score, GridSearchCV
from sklearn.metrics import accuracy_score, precision_score, recall_score, f1_score, roc_auc_score
In [24]:
numeric_pipeline = Pipeline(
    steps=[
        ("imputer", SimpleImputer(strategy="median")),
        ("scaler", StandardScaler())
    ]
)
In [25]:
categorical_pipeline = Pipeline(
    steps=[
        ("imputer", SimpleImputer(strategy="most_frequent")),
        ("encoder", OneHotEncoder(handle_unknown="ignore"))
    ]
)

Numerical features use median imputation and StandardScaler. Categorical features use most-frequent imputation and OneHotEncoder. ColumnTransformer keeps preprocessing inside the training pipeline, improving reproducibility and reducing leakage.

In [26]:
preprocessor = ColumnTransformer(
    transformers=[
        ("num", numeric_pipeline, numeric_features),
        ("cat", categorical_pipeline, categorical_features)
    ]
)

6. Data Engineering Comparison¶

Three LR strategies are compared: A imputation+encoding, B A+scaling, C B+SelectKBest.

In [27]:
numeric_pipeline_a = Pipeline([
    ("imputer", SimpleImputer(strategy="median"))
])

categorical_pipeline_a = Pipeline([
    ("imputer", SimpleImputer(strategy="most_frequent")),
    ("encoder", OneHotEncoder(handle_unknown="ignore"))
])

preprocessor_a = ColumnTransformer([
    ("num", numeric_pipeline_a, numeric_features),
    ("cat", categorical_pipeline_a, categorical_features)
])

pipeline_a = Pipeline([
    ("preprocessor", preprocessor_a),
    ("model", LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000, random_state=RANDOM_STATE))
])

pipeline_b = Pipeline([
    ("preprocessor", preprocessor),
    ("model", LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000, random_state=RANDOM_STATE))
])

pipeline_c = Pipeline([
    ("preprocessor", preprocessor),
    ("selector", SelectKBest(score_func=f_classif, k=10)),
    ("model", LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000, random_state=RANDOM_STATE))
])

engineering_pipelines = {
    "A: No Scaling": pipeline_a,
    "B: Scaling": pipeline_b,
    "C: Scaling + SelectKBest": pipeline_c
}

engineering_results = []
for name, pipe in engineering_pipelines.items():
    pipe.fit(X_train, y_train)
    pred = pipe.predict(X_test)
    engineering_results.append({"Strategy": name, "F1 Score": f1_score(y_test, pred)})

comparison_df = pd.DataFrame(engineering_results)
comparison_df
Out[27]:
Strategy F1 Score
0 A: No Scaling 0.740684
1 B: Scaling 0.764618
2 C: Scaling + SelectKBest 0.462273
In [28]:
plt.figure(figsize=(7,4))
sns.barplot(data=comparison_df, x="Strategy", y="F1 Score")
plt.title("Preprocessing Strategy Comparison")
plt.xticks(rotation=15)
plt.show()
In [29]:
feature_to_scale = "temperature_motor"
raw_feature = X_train[[feature_to_scale]]
imputed = SimpleImputer(strategy="median").fit_transform(raw_feature)
scaled = StandardScaler().fit_transform(imputed)
scaling_demo_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "Raw": imputed.ravel(),
    "Scaled": scaled.ravel()
})

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(9,3))
sns.histplot(scaling_demo_df["Raw"], ax=axes[0], bins=30)
axes[0].set_title("Before Scaling")
sns.histplot(scaling_demo_df["Scaled"], ax=axes[1], bins=30)
axes[1].set_title("After StandardScaler")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Data Engineering Results¶

Scaling improved LR F1 from 0.741 to 0.765. SelectKBest reduced F1 to 0.462, so feature selection removed useful signals and was not selected.

7. Model Training¶

In [30]:
models = {
    "Logistic Regression": LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000, random_state=RANDOM_STATE),
    "Decision Tree": DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=RANDOM_STATE),
    "Random Forest": RandomForestClassifier(random_state=RANDOM_STATE),
    "Gradient Boosting": GradientBoostingClassifier(random_state=RANDOM_STATE)
}
In [31]:
model_results = []

for model_name, model in models.items():
    model_pipeline = Pipeline([
        ("preprocessor", preprocessor),
        ("model", model)
    ])

    cv_scores = cross_val_score(
        model_pipeline,
        X_train,
        y_train,
        cv=5,
        scoring="f1",
        n_jobs=1
    )

    model_pipeline.fit(X_train, y_train)
    y_pred = model_pipeline.predict(X_test)
    y_proba = model_pipeline.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1]

    model_results.append({
        "Model": model_name,
        "CV F1 Mean": cv_scores.mean(),
        "CV F1 Std": cv_scores.std(),
        "Accuracy": accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred),
        "Precision": precision_score(y_test, y_pred),
        "Recall": recall_score(y_test, y_pred),
        "F1 Score": f1_score(y_test, y_pred),
        "ROC-AUC": roc_auc_score(y_test, y_proba)
    })

model_results_df = pd.DataFrame(model_results)
model_results_df.sort_values(by="F1 Score", ascending=False)
Out[31]:
Model CV F1 Mean CV F1 Std Accuracy Precision Recall F1 Score ROC-AUC
2 Random Forest 0.919785 0.005900 0.973383 0.912429 0.907303 0.909859 0.995917
1 Decision Tree 0.845499 0.006679 0.954668 0.844011 0.851124 0.847552 0.911893
3 Gradient Boosting 0.835211 0.005797 0.951549 0.862330 0.800562 0.830299 0.981871
0 Logistic Regression 0.754307 0.011423 0.934706 0.819936 0.716292 0.764618 0.963600

Initial Model Comparison¶

Models use 5-fold CV plus held-out test scores. Random Forest performs best.

In [32]:
plt.figure(figsize=(8,5))

sns.barplot(
    data=model_results_df.sort_values(
        by="F1 Score",
        ascending=False
    ),
    x="Model",
    y="F1 Score"
)

plt.title("Model Comparison by F1 Score")
plt.xticks(rotation=20)

plt.show()

F1 is the main metric because failures are the minority class.

8. Hyperparameter Tuning¶

Grid search tunes LR (C, penalty, solver) and RF (n_estimators, depth, split size, max_features).

In [33]:
rf_pipeline = Pipeline(
    steps=[
        ("preprocessor", preprocessor),
        ("model", RandomForestClassifier(random_state=RANDOM_STATE))
    ]
)
In [34]:
param_grid = {
    "model__n_estimators": [100, 200],
    "model__max_depth": [10, None],
    "model__min_samples_split": [2, 5],
    "model__max_features": ["sqrt", "log2"]
}
In [35]:
lr_pipeline = Pipeline([
    ("preprocessor", preprocessor),
    ("model", LogisticRegression(max_iter=1000, random_state=RANDOM_STATE))
])

lr_param_grid = {
    "model__C": [0.01, 0.1, 1, 10, 100],
    "model__penalty": ["l2"],
    "model__solver": ["lbfgs", "liblinear"]
}

lr_grid_search = GridSearchCV(
    estimator=lr_pipeline,
    param_grid=lr_param_grid,
    cv=5,
    scoring="f1",
    n_jobs=1
)

_ = lr_grid_search.fit(X_train, y_train)

print("Best Parameters:")
print(lr_grid_search.best_params_)
print("Best F1 Score:")
print(lr_grid_search.best_score_)

Best Parameters:
{'model__C': 100, 'model__penalty': 'l2', 'model__solver': 'liblinear'}
Best F1 Score:
0.7567764722840998

Logistic Regression Hyperparameter Tuning¶

C controls regularisation strength, while solver changes the optimisation method. The grid checks whether weaker regularisation improves recall without overfitting.

In [36]:
grid_search = GridSearchCV(
    estimator=rf_pipeline,
    param_grid=param_grid,
    cv=5,
    scoring="f1",
    n_jobs=1
)

_ = grid_search.fit(X_train, y_train)
In [37]:
print("Best Parameters:")
print(grid_search.best_params_)

print("\nBest F1 Score:")
print(grid_search.best_score_)

Best Parameters:
{'model__max_depth': None, 'model__max_features': 'sqrt', 'model__min_samples_split': 2, 'model__n_estimators': 200}

Best F1 Score:
0.91983869978633
In [38]:
best_rf = grid_search.best_estimator_

y_pred_tuned = best_rf.predict(X_test)
y_proba_tuned = best_rf.predict_proba(X_test)[:, 1]

final_metrics = {
    "Accuracy": accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred_tuned),
    "Precision": precision_score(y_test, y_pred_tuned),
    "Recall": recall_score(y_test, y_pred_tuned),
    "F1 Score": f1_score(y_test, y_pred_tuned),
    "ROC-AUC": roc_auc_score(y_test, y_proba_tuned)
}

final_metrics_df = pd.DataFrame([final_metrics])
final_metrics_df
Out[38]:
Accuracy Precision Recall F1 Score ROC-AUC
0 0.974631 0.922636 0.904494 0.913475 0.996044
In [39]:
best_rf_model = grid_search.best_estimator_

feature_names = best_rf_model.named_steps["preprocessor"].get_feature_names_out()

importances = best_rf_model.named_steps["model"].feature_importances_

feature_importance_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "Feature": feature_names,
    "Importance": importances
}).sort_values(by="Importance", ascending=False)

feature_importance_df.head(15)

In [40]:
plt.figure(figsize=(10,6))

sns.barplot(
    data=feature_importance_df.head(15),
    x="Importance",
    y="Feature"
)

plt.title("Top 15 Feature Importances - Random Forest")
plt.xlabel("Importance")
plt.ylabel("Feature")

plt.show()

Feature Importance Analysis¶

Random Forest feature importances improve explainability. The strongest predictors are operational sensor variables such as motor temperature, RPM, vibration, current, maintenance history and pressure.

Hyperparameter Tuning Results¶

In [41]:
cv_results = pd.DataFrame(grid_search.cv_results_)

plt.figure(figsize=(7,4))
sns.lineplot(
    data=cv_results,
    x="param_model__n_estimators",
    y="mean_test_score",
    hue="param_model__max_depth",
    marker="o"
)
plt.title("Random Forest Hyperparameter Effect")
plt.ylabel("Mean CV F1 Score")
plt.xlabel("Number of Trees")
plt.show()

RF selected 200 trees, no depth limit, split size 2 and max_features='sqrt'. This gave CV F1 = 0.920 and test F1 = 0.913.

Class Imbalance Check¶

Because failures are only about 15% of the data, a balanced Logistic Regression baseline is tested against the regular model.

In [42]:
balanced_lr = Pipeline([
    ("preprocessor", preprocessor),
    ("model", LogisticRegression(
        max_iter=1000,
        class_weight="balanced",
        random_state=RANDOM_STATE
    ))
])

balanced_lr.fit(X_train, y_train)
balanced_pred = balanced_lr.predict(X_test)

imbalance_df = pd.DataFrame([
    {"Model": "Logistic Regression", "F1 Score": f1_score(y_test, pipeline_b.predict(X_test))},
    {"Model": "Balanced Logistic Regression", "F1 Score": f1_score(y_test, balanced_pred)}
])
imbalance_df
Out[42]:
Model F1 Score
0 Logistic Regression 0.764618
1 Balanced Logistic Regression 0.760186

The balanced LR test gave F1 = 0.760 versus 0.765 for regular LR, so class weighting did not improve this dataset. RF remains the final model.

Loss Function Curve¶

Gradient Boosting training loss is plotted by boosting stage to directly show optimisation behaviour.

In [43]:
gbm_loss_pipeline = Pipeline([
    ("preprocessor", preprocessor),
    ("model", GradientBoostingClassifier(random_state=RANDOM_STATE))
])

gbm_loss_pipeline.fit(X_train, y_train)
train_loss = gbm_loss_pipeline.named_steps["model"].train_score_

plt.figure(figsize=(6,4))
plt.plot(range(1, len(train_loss) + 1), train_loss, marker="o", markersize=3)
plt.title("Gradient Boosting Training Loss")
plt.xlabel("Boosting Stage")
plt.ylabel("Deviance Loss")
plt.show()

The loss decreases and then flattens, so later boosting stages add limited extra improvement. This supports using validation metrics, not training loss alone, for final model choice.

9. Final Evaluation¶

In [44]:
from sklearn.metrics import confusion_matrix

cm = confusion_matrix(y_test, y_pred_tuned)

plt.figure(figsize=(5,4))
sns.heatmap(cm, annot=True, fmt="d", cmap="Blues")
plt.title("Confusion Matrix")
plt.xlabel("Predicted")
plt.ylabel("Actual")
plt.show()

false_negatives = cm[1, 0]
false_positives = cm[0, 1]
print("False negatives:", false_negatives)
print("False positives:", false_positives)

False negatives: 68
False positives: 54

10. Visualization and Discussion¶

Confusion Matrix Interpretation¶

The tuned Random Forest correctly identifies most normal and failure cases. The remaining errors are important in business terms: false negatives mean missed failures, while false positives mean unnecessary maintenance checks.

In [45]:
from sklearn.metrics import roc_curve

fpr, tpr, thresholds = roc_curve(y_test, y_proba_tuned)

plt.figure(figsize=(6,4))
plt.plot(fpr, tpr, label=f"AUC = {final_metrics['ROC-AUC']:.4f}")
plt.plot([0,1], [0,1], linestyle="--")
plt.xlabel("False Positive Rate")
plt.ylabel("True Positive Rate")
plt.title("ROC Curve")
plt.legend()
plt.show()

ROC Curve Interpretation¶

The ROC curve is close to the upper-left corner and the AUC is high, showing strong separation between failure and non-failure observations.

11. Conclusion¶

The final Random Forest is the strongest model, with test F1 = 0.913 and ROC-AUC = 0.996 after leakage prevention, preprocessing comparison, 5-fold CV and tuning.

The most informative features are temperature, RPM, vibration, current and hours since maintenance. This fits the business problem: stressed machines are more likely to fail.

Limitations¶

The analysis uses one public, structured dataset, so real production performance may be lower. Random splitting can place records from the same machine in both train and test sets, which may overestimate generalisation. False negatives remain the main risk.

Future Work¶

Before deployment, I would pilot the model on recent company data, compare predictions with maintenance logs, monitor false negatives, drift and calibration, and keep human review for high-risk alerts. Future validation should use time-based or machine-based splits.

12. References¶

Kaggle. (2025). Industrial Machine Predictive Maintenance Dataset. Available at: https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/tatheerabbas/industrial-machine-predictive-maintenance

Pedregosa, F. et al. (2011). Scikit-learn: Machine Learning in Python. Journal of Machine Learning Research, 12, pp. 2825-2830.

McKinney, W. (2022). Python for Data Analysis. 3rd ed. O'Reilly Media.

Hunter, J.D. (2007). Matplotlib: A 2D Graphics Environment. Computing in Science & Engineering, 9(3), pp. 90-95.
